planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Henderson is very sensitive to cold soil and seed will rot rather than germinate in cold wet ground — be patient and wait for warm conditions.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep directly in warm soil (above 65°F) after last frost in full sun, in fertile, well-drained soil; Henderson Bush Lima is the standard small-seeded bush lima bean, an heirloom selection dating to the 1880s that remains the most widely grown small white lima because of its outstanding heat tolerance, compact growth requiring no staking, and prolific production of small, tender, buttery beans in a compressed time frame. The small seed size means faster cooking than large limas and a creamier texture; it is excellent fresh, frozen, or dried as a dry bean. Space rows 18–24 inches apart for good air circulation and reduce disease pressure. Mulch to keep soil moist as pods develop.

common_name: Christmas Lima Bean (Speckled Lima, Chestnut Lima)

planting.note: Sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Christmas Lima is a large-seeded pole type needing 5–6 feet of trellis support; plant along fences or provide sturdy poles.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil (above 65°F) after last frost at the base of a sturdy trellis, fence, or poles in full sun, in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Christmas Lima is a striking heirloom pole lima with large seeds dramatically streaked and mottled in red-maroon and cream — the colors fade somewhat on cooking but the flavor more than compensates, often described as chestnut-like with a rich, buttery texture superior to common limas. A long-season variety that rewards gardeners in warm climates; in zone 9–10, the extended growing season produces heavy yields late into fall. Dry the seeds for storage as beautiful speckled beans that are prized for soups, stews, and succotash. Requires cross-pollination and benefits from hand removal of diseased pods.

common_name: Calypso Bean (Yin Yang Bean, Oreo Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; a bush bean requiring no support — excellent for market gardeners and home canners.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Calypso is a bush dry bean with a striking black and white two-toned pattern — each seed is precisely half black, half white — that has earned it nicknames such as 'yin yang bean' and 'oreo bean' for the dramatic visual contrast. The pattern fades somewhat on cooking but the bean retains excellent earthy, full-bodied flavor with a smooth creamy texture when fully cooked. Among the fastest dry beans to reach maturity, making it suitable for short-season gardens; the compact bush habit produces a concentrated flush excellent for preserving. Color fades on drying — store in a dark, cool location to preserve seed appearance.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 60°F; a bush bean needing no support; pick pods frequently if harvesting as a snap bean.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Tiger Eye is a stunning heirloom bush bean with large seeds in a rich golden-yellow to tan background covered with auburn to deep brown streaking and marbling — the pattern resembles a tiger's eye gemstone and retains some color on cooking. The flavor is particularly rich and buttery for a dry bean, often described as creamy with a hint of chestnut, making it excellent in soups and stews or as a simple side dish. A productive and reliable bush bean that dries beautifully on the plant and stores well.

common_name: Rio Zape Bean (Hopi Purple String Bean, Ancient Aztec Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; a pole bean requiring 5–6 feet of support; drought-adapted from its desert Southwest origins, requiring less water than many beans once established.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost at the base of sturdy poles or trellises in full sun; Rio Zape is an ancient bean of Aztec and Hopi origin with distinctive dark purple-brown seeds streaked and spotted with maroon or lavender markings that fade to a deep chocolate color on cooking. It has an exceptional rich, chocolatey-earthy flavor with complex undertones — often called the best-tasting dry bean by chefs who discover it — and a creamy texture excellent in tacos, soups, and refried beans. Drought-adapted from its desert Southwest heritage; it performs well in hot, dry conditions where other beans struggle. A prized heirloom for serious bean enthusiasts.

common_name: Jacob's Cattle Bean (Trout Bean, Forellen Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; a bush bean suitable for mechanized harvesting and home gardens alike; grown in Maine since the 1800s for use in traditional New England baked beans.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Jacob's Cattle is a New England heirloom dry bean documented in Maine since at least the mid-1800s, with white seeds dramatically splashed and dotted with deep red-maroon that makes each seed look painted, earning it the alternate name 'trout bean' (for the spotted resemblance to brook trout) and the German name 'Forellen' (trout). The flavor is mild, earthy, and creamy when cooked — the classic bean for old-fashioned New England baked beans where its thick skin holds shape through long cooking. Also excellent in soups and stews. The color fades during cooking but the pattern is striking on dried seeds; a visually beautiful bean for the market table.
sources: Maine Organic Farmers and Gardeners Association
